title: Sudden confusion and sweating in a diabetic

chart.oneLiner: 68M with diabetes is brought in by family for sudden confusion, sweating, and tremor that began before dinner.

opening: I… I don't feel right. I'm all shaky and sweaty.
patientFile:
PERSONA: 68-year-old man, diaphoretic, tremulous, confused and slow but rousable. Poor historian during the episode.

COLLATERAL (provide ONLY if the student asks family/EMS/chart/medication list): Family says he became confused and sweaty about 30 minutes ago. He takes insulin and a sulfonylurea (glipizide) for diabetes, gave his usual insulin this morning but skipped lunch because he wasn't hungry. No fever, no trauma, no chest pain. Recently his kidney function has been worsening. MEDS: insulin glargine, glipizide, lisinopril.

HPI (from patient — limited): feels shaky, sweaty, 'foggy.' Can't give clear timeline.
PMH: type 2 diabetes, chronic kidney disease. SOCIAL: lives with spouse.

PHYSICAL EXAM (report ONLY what is requested; give normals when asked):
- General: diaphoretic, tremulous, anxious, confused but arousable.
- Vitals: BP 142/86, HR 104, RR 18, 36.8°C, SpO2 98% RA.
- Neuro: confused; may have transient focal findings that resolve with treatment; no neck stiffness.
- Cardiac: tachycardic, regular. Skin: cool and diaphoretic.

HIDDEN RULES:
- NEVER state or hint at a diagnosis.
- The patient is confused: vague answers. Provide COLLATERAL (insulin + sulfonylurea use, skipped meal, CKD) only when the student explicitly asks family/medication list/what happened.
- If asked something not in this file, give a reasonable normal/negative answer.

labs.Glucose: Fingerstick glucose 38 mg/dL — LOW (the key test).
labs.BMP: Na 138, K 4.4, BUN 40, Cr 2.2 (CKD — prolongs sulfonylurea effect), Glu 38.

ekg.label: 12-lead EKG
ekg.findings: ST depression, flat T waves, prominent U waves, long QU.
ekg.verify: Source a study matching: ST depression, flat T waves, prominent U waves, long QU. Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
ekg.attribution: LITFL ECG Library (litfl.com) — © Life in the Fast Lane, CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. Educational/not-for-profit use, credit litfl.com; skip any image labelled third-party.

cxr.label: Chest X-ray
cxr.findings: Curvilinear azygos fissure with a teardrop azygos vein in the right upper zone (benign variant).
cxr.verify: Source a study matching: Curvilinear azygos fissure with a teardrop azygos vein in the right upper zone (benign variant). Self-host with appropriate credit; do not reuse the prior normal tracing.
cxr.imageDescription: chest radiograph showing azygos lobe fissure

steps.label: Differential
steps.prompt: Based on your assessment, list your differential (note your leading diagnosis).
steps.rubric: Hypoglycemia (leading & CANT-MISS,9; the adrenergic + neuroglycopenic picture in a treated diabetic). Stroke/TIA (CANT-MISS,4). Seizure/postictal (core,3). Intoxication (core,2). Sepsis (core,2). Cardiac arrhythmia (bonus,2). Names hypoglycemia and the imperative to check glucose immediately (core,3).
steps.idealAnswer: Leading diagnosis: Hypoglycemia. Must-not-miss diagnoses to exclude: Stroke/TIA. Also reasonable: Seizure/postictal; Intoxication; Sepsis.

coreActions.item: Names hypoglycemia and the imperative to check glucose immediately

steps.label: Workup
steps.prompt: What is the single most important immediate test, and what else do you order?
steps.rubric: IMMEDIATE fingerstick glucose (CANT-MISS,7). BMP incl. renal function (3). CBC (2). EKG (2). Considers checking for a sulfonylurea/insulin cause (1). PENALTY: ordering a CT head BEFORE checking a glucose in an obviously hypoglycemic-appearing diabetic (-3).

penalties.item: ordering a CT head BEFORE checking a glucose in an obviously hypoglycemic-appearing diabetic

contraindicatedTests: ordering a CT head BEFORE checking a glucose in an obviously hypoglycemic-appearing diabetic
orderSensitiveActions: ordering a CT head BEFORE checking a glucose in an obviously hypoglycemic-appearing diabetic

steps.label: Read EKG
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's 12-lead EKG. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = sinus tachycardia, no acute ischemia. CREDIT: rate & rhythm (7); no acute ischemic changes (5); systematic read (3). PENALTY: over-calling ischemia (-3).
keyFindings: ST depression / T-wave flattening of hypokalemia
commonMistakes: calling the U waves part of the T wave and overestimating QT
steps.idealAnswer: Hypokalemia: ST depression, flattened T waves, prominent U waves.

coreActions: ST depression / T-wave flattening of hypokalemia

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Read CXR
steps.prompt: Here is the patient's chest X-ray. Describe your findings.
steps.rubric: Findings = no acute process. CREDIT: identifies a normal film (6); notes low diagnostic yield / only to exclude aspiration (4). PENALTY: inventing pathology (-2).
keyFindings: recognizes it as a benign anatomic variant
commonMistakes: mistaking the azygos fissure for a pneumothorax line or scar
steps.idealAnswer: Azygos lobe: a curvilinear fissure with a teardrop azygos vein — a benign anatomic variant.

coreActions: recognizes it as a benign anatomic variant

penalties.label: calls the study normal / misses the abnormality

steps.label: Revised Dx
steps.prompt: The glucose is 38. What is your diagnosis and why is this patient at particular risk?
steps.rubric: Hypoglycemia (6). Identifies the mechanism — insulin + sulfonylurea with a skipped meal and worsening CKD prolonging drug effect (4).

steps.label: Management
steps.rubric: Per MGH inpatient glycemic management: immediate treatment – PO carbohydrate or IV dextrose (D50)/IM glucagon (CANT-MISS,8); recheck glucose in 15 min and repeat (4); identify and treat cause – insulin/sulfonylurea/missed meal/renal/EtOH (5); hold offending agent (3); consider octreotide for sulfonylurea overdose (2); give thiamine before glucose if alcohol use suspected (1). PENALTY: failing to recheck glucose (-2); discharging sulfonylurea hypoglycemia early (-2).
steps.idealAnswer: Treat hypoglycemia immediately: if able to take PO, 15–20 g fast carbohydrate; if unable/severe, IV dextrose (D50) or IM glucagon. Recheck glucose in 15 minutes and repeat until >70–80. Identify and treat the cause (insulin/sulfonylurea, missed meal, renal failure, alcohol). Hold the offending agent; for sulfonylurea-induced, observe and consider octreotide.

coreActions.item: Recheck glucose in 15 min and repeat

bonusActions.item: Thiamine before glucose if alcohol use

optionalActions: Recheck glucose in 15 min and repeat
Thiamine before glucose if alcohol use

caseSummary.diagnosticFramework: 68M with diabetes is brought in by family for sudden confusion, sweating, and tremor that began before dinner. The diagnosis is Hypoglycemia, reached by eliciting the focused history, performing the targeted exam, and interpreting the directed workup while excluding the must-not-miss alternatives.
keyPhysicalExamFindings: Neuro: confused; may have transient focal findings that resolve with treatment; no neck stiffness.
General: diaphoretic, tremulous, anxious, confused but arousable.
General: diaphoretic, tremulous, anxious, confused but arousable.
reasoningPathway: Frame the presentation from the chart and vitals.
Elicit the focused history, including sensitive items only on direct questioning.
Perform the targeted physical exam maneuvers to reveal key findings.
Build a differential led by Hypoglycemia while explicitly excluding must-not-miss causes.
Order a focused, order-sensitive workup and interpret EKG/CXR and labs.
Commit to Hypoglycemia and manage with stabilization, definitive treatment, consults, and disposition.
criticalInterpretationItems: Sinus tachycardia; no acute ischemic changes (hypoglycemia can occasionally provoke ischemia).
No acute cardiopulmonary process (low yield here; obtained if aspiration suspected).
imageInterpretationPearls: An incidental abnormality is an interpretation task in its own right — name it, then judge its relevance.
Read every study systematically before committing.
State pertinent negatives, not just positives.

items.item: Shows empathy for the patient's concern
Explains next steps in patient-friendly language
